preview-before-update-delete.label: Preview before update/delete
preview-before-update-delete.summary: Learn how to safely preview the rows affected by UPDATE and DELETE statements in SQL before making changes, using the inventory_items table.

code-preview-select-before-update.title: Preview rows before updating
code-preview-select-before-update.prompt: You want to update the status of all items in the 'Accessories' category to 'inactive'. Write a SQL statement to preview which rows will be affected.
code-preview-select-before-update.hint: Focus on the concept being tested.

help.concept: Think about the role or idea being tested rather than repeating answer wording.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.

code-preview-select-before-delete.title: Preview rows before deleting
code-preview-select-before-delete.prompt: Before deleting all test items (where is_test = 1), write a SQL statement to preview which rows will be deleted.
code-preview-select-before-delete.hint: Focus on the concept being tested.

help.concept: Think about the role or idea being tested rather than repeating answer wording.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.

code-update-after-preview.title: Update after previewing
code-update-after-preview.prompt: After previewing, update all items in the 'Kitchen' category to set their status to 'inactive'. Write the SQL UPDATE statement.
code-update-after-preview.hint: Focus on the SQL task being asked for, not on copying final query text.

help.concept: Build the query from the operation the exercise is testing.
help.hint_1: Think about which clauses or functions are required for the task.
help.hint_2: Construct the query based on what result the exercise expects, not by repeating exact solution wording.

multi-why-preview-important.title: Why is previewing before UPDATE or DELETE important?
multi-why-preview-important.prompt: Select all reasons why previewing affected rows before running UPDATE or DELETE is important.
multi-why-preview-important.hint: Think about risks and benefits of previewing.

help.concept: Previewing helps prevent mistakes and data loss by showing you which rows will be affected.
help.hint_1: Consider both data safety and error prevention.
help.hint_2: Previewing helps avoid accidental changes and lets you confirm your WHERE clause.

fill-blank-preview-where.title: Key clause for safe updates/deletes
fill-blank-preview-where.prompt: Choose the best value for the first missing blank in the statement.
fill-blank-preview-where.hint: This clause filters which rows are affected.

help.concept: The the missing term clause specifies which rows to update or delete, preventing changes to all rows.
help.hint_1: It's the clause that limits the affected rows.
help.hint_2: Without this clause, all rows would be updated or deleted.

sketch-preview-select.title: Why preview before changing data?
sketch-preview-select.bodyMarkdown:
When you run an UPDATE or DELETE statement in SQL, you are making permanent changes to your data. Unlike SELECT, these changes can't be undone with a simple command. That's why it's a best practice to **preview** which rows will be affected before you run the actual UPDATE or DELETE.

For example, if you want to update the status of all test items in the `inventory_items` table, first run a SELECT with the same WHERE clause:

```sql
SELECT * FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

This shows you exactly which rows will be changed. Once you're sure, you can run:

```sql
UPDATE inventory_items SET status = 'archived' WHERE is_test = 1;
```

Previewing helps prevent accidental data loss or unwanted changes.

sketch-preview-delete.title: Preview before deleting
sketch-preview-delete.bodyMarkdown:
Deleting data is even riskier than updating it, because deleted rows are gone for good unless you have a backup. Always preview which rows will be deleted by running a SELECT statement with the same WHERE clause first.

For example, to delete all test items:

```sql
SELECT * FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

If the results look correct, then run:

```sql
DELETE FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

This habit helps you avoid deleting the wrong data by mistake.
